13.reason: This reads any character including spaces. Since the expression may contain spaces, it should skip whitespace before reading the operator. Use `scanf(" %c",&fu[i]);` to consume leading whitespace.

reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-1: 1.0 , reason:  This reads any character including spaces. Since the expression may contain spaces, it should skip whitespace before reading the operator. Use `scanf(" %c",&fu[i]);` to consume leading whitespace.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-1: 1.0 , reason:  The format string "%c" reads any character, including spaces and newlines. Since the input expression contains spaces (e.g., "10 + 20"), this will read a space into fu[i] instead of the operator '+'. This causes the logic to fail because fu[i] will not match '*', '/', '+', or '-', leading to incorrect parsing of subsequent numbers and operators. It should be " %c" (with a leading space) to skip whitespace, or the input handling should be adjusted to ignore spaces.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 1.0 , reason:  The format string "%c" reads any character, including whitespace (spaces, newlines). Since the input expression contains spaces (e.g., "10 + 20"), this will read a space into fu[i] instead of the operator '+'. This causes the logic to fail because fu[i] will not match '*', '/', '+', or '-', leading to incorrect parsing of subsequent numbers and operators. It should be " %c" (with a leading space) to skip whitespace, or the input handling should be adjusted to ignore spaces.
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This reads a single character including whitespace (like space after number), which may cause misalignment between operators and numbers. The input "10 + 20 * 30 - 40 / 5 =" has spaces, so `scanf("%c")` will read the space instead of '+' first.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This reads a single character, but may read whitespace (like space) before the operator. The input has spaces, so this causes misalignment between operators and numbers.]

31.reason: [Indexing error: `j` starts at 1, but when the first operator is '+' or '-', `j` increments and `t[j-1]` refers to correct value. However, due to earlier input parsing issue, `fu[i]` might not correspond to the intended operator, leading to wrong operations or skipped terms. Also, `num[i]` may be uninitialized or misaligned because of incorrect parsing.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Indexing error: when encountering '*' or '/', it uses t[j-1] which assumes j was incremented for every number, but j is only incremented for '+'/'-'. This leads to incorrect indexing and overwriting wrong values.]
